abstract: In the present study, cromolyn sodium (CS), a highly water soluble molecule was encapsulated into rationally designed, core-shell polymeric-lipid hybrid nanoparticles (PLHNs) for enhancing its oral bioavailability, by improving its intestinal permeability through lymphatic uptake. The CS encapsulated PLGA-lecithin based core-shell PLHNs (CS-PLHNs) were engineered by a double emulsification solvent evaporation method and optimized using a response surface methodology based "Quality by Design" approach. The Box-Behnken experimental design was imperatively enforced to enhance encapsulation of CS inside PLHNs without compromising particle size. Optimized CS-PLHNs exhibited a particle size of 227 +- 3.8 nm and EE of 57.8 +- 1.32% with unimodal size distribution. The physico-chemical characterizations of CS-PLHNs suggested the encapsulation of CS in an amorphous form inside PLHNs without any interactions. The morphological studies pointed towards the existence of smooth, spherical core-shell architecture of CS-PLHNs, which extended release up to 48 h by a controlled diffusion process. The optimized CS-PLHNs exhibited remarkable stability at different environmental conditions as well as in biological milieu. An ex vivo intestinal permeation study showed that the permeation of CS was significantly improved by encapsulating it inside PLHNs compared to that of pure CS, which was additionally confirmed by the in vivo intestinal uptake study using confocal microscopy. A pharmacokinetic study in rats further exhibited 11.9 fold enhancement in the oral bioavailability of CS after its incorporation into PLHNs. In a nutshell, PLHNs can serve as a superior therapeutic carrier system by imparting lipophilicity to potentially obtain the high oral bioavailability of CS, which can further be extended towards numerous hydrophilic drug molecules.
conclusion:
The present strategy provides a deep insight into the captivating features of PLHNs for oral delivery of CS like water soluble molecules. The W1/O/W2 double emulsification method was successfully employed for encapsulation of CS into PLHNs. By virtue of Box-Behnken experimental design based Quality by Design approach, enormous efforts have been made for higher drug encapsulation. The high degree of prediction and realization potential confirmed the utility of desirability approach for optimization. The various solid-state and surface characterization studies exhibited the core-shell architecture of PLHNs, in which CS was resided in an amorphous form without any interaction. The absorption and permeation of CS in rat intestinal epithelial barrier improved greatly after its incorporation inside PLHNs. Eventually, in vivo intestinal uptake and pharmacokinetic study confirmed the intestinal permeation potential and thereby, its resultant enhanced bioavailability, which ought to be the lipophilicity imparted by PLHNs. Further, PLHNs offer an exciting delivery strategy for altering the existing marketed dosage form of CS as a result of its improved oral bioavailability. In the purview of this, the present research opens new era in the pharmaceutical field for harnessing the potential of CS like numerous water soluble drug molecules, by extrapolating the intriguing findings of tunable PLHNs.
Conflict of interest
The authors report no conflict of interest.
Abbreviations
materials_methods:
Cromolyn sodium (CS) and PLGA Resomer(r) RG 750S (poly(D,L-lactide-co-glycolide) 75:25; MW: 75000-115000 Da) were obtained as gift sample from Entod Pharmaceuticals (Mumbai, India) and Evonik Industries, Pvt. Ltd, Mumbai, India, respectively. Lecithin and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (MW 80k-125 kDa) were purchased from Himedia labs, Mumbai, India. Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. All other chemicals and solvents used during the experiments were of analytical grade and procured from SD fine Chemicals, Mumbai, India. Double Distilled Water (DDW) was used throughout the experimentations.
2.2 Preparation of CS encapsulated PLHNs
PLGA-lecithin based core-shell CS-PLHNs were prepared by modified double emulsification solvent evaporation method (W1/O/W2) as earlier reported by Patel et al.23 In brief, 25 mg of CS was dissolved in 0.5 ml of DDW (W1) while weighed amount of PLGA was dissolved in dichloromethane (O) containing 2% w/v span 80. The external aqueous phase (W2) was prepared by dispersing designated amount of lecithin and PVA in DDW containing acetic acid (0.1% v/v) and tetrahydrofuran (4% v/v). The internal aqueous phase (W1) was added in an organic phase (O) and emulsified by applying 80% sonication amplitude for 90 s using ultra probe sonicator (UP50H, Hielscher, USA) over an ice bath. The formed primary emulsion (W1/O) was poured into the external aqueous phase (W2) under continuous stirring and then whole mixture was sonicated over an ice bath, using ultra probe sonicator for 5 min. The resultant emulsion (W1/O/W2) was allowed to stir for 6 h on magnetic stirrer at 1000 rpm. CS-PLHNs were concentrated by removing organic phase and excess water, under reduced pressure using rotatory evaporator (IKA(r) RV10, Germany). The prepared CS-PLHNs were recovered by centrifugation at 15000 rpm using cooling centrifuge (RC 4100 F, Eltek, Mumbai, India) for 15 min at 4 degC temperature. The supernatant was separated and kept aside for drug content analysis as described later. The particles, gathered in the form of sediment were washed three times and then resuspended in a fixed volume of DDW. The CS-PLHNs were lyophilized with mannitol (2% w/v) using freeze dryer (Labconco, USA) for 48 h at -45 degC and 0.050 mbar pressure. For, solid state characterizations, CS-PLHNs were lyophilized without mannitol intentionally, in order to avoid interference of mannitol peaks with pure CS peaks. The dried CS-PLHNs were stored in desiccator till further use.

The Quality by Design approach was performed systematically by employing response surface methodology (RSM) for constructing and analyzing second-order polynomial models, with a small number of experimental runs. A 4-factor, 3-level BBD was used to investigate the quadratic response surfaces by evaluating the influence of independent variables on response variables i.e., particle size (Y1), encapsulation efficiency (EE) (Y2) and PDI (Y3) of CS-PLHNs. The lipid/polymer ratio (X1), concentration of surfactant (X2) and organic phase/aqueous phase ratio (X3) and concentration of polymer (X4) were chosen as four independent variables on the basis of the preliminary screening, using Placket-Burman screening design.23 Each variable is varied at three different levels. Each variable is represented as +1, 0 and -1, analogous to their high, medium and low levels, respectively, which were determined from preliminary experimentation. All independent variables and response variables along with their actual and coded levels are described in Table 1. The generation and statistical analysis of experimental design matrix comprising of 29 runs, including 24 factorial point and 5 center points was executed by using Design-Expert(r) software (8.0.6.1, Stat-Ease Inc., Minneapolis, USA). The correlation between two independent variables and response variable was delineated by response surface 3D plots. All experiments were run in a randomized order to avoid any possible source of experimental bias and to increase the predictability of the model.21,24 The non-linear polynomial equation generated by the BBD is given as
where, Y is the measured response of the dependent variable associated with each factor level combination; b0 is an intercept; b1-b14 are the regression coefficients of the respective variables; X1-X4 are the coded levels correspond to the independent variables; the term XiXi and Xi2 (i = 1, 2, 3 or 4) represent the interactive and quadratic terms of the independent variables, respectively and E is the random error.25
Table 1 Independent variables with their levels and dependent variables with their constraints in Box-Behnken experimental design
Data were analyzed by employing multiple linear regressions. The correlation coefficient (R2) and lack of fit value were determined in order to find out adequacy of the regression model. Further, optimization was performed by employing numerical optimization based desirability technique.
2.4 Characterizations of CS-PLHNs
2.4.1 Particle size, polydispersity index (PDI) and zeta potential. Measurement of particle size, zeta potential and PDI of prepared CS-PLHNs was carried out by photon correlation spectroscopy using Delsa Nano C particle size analyzer (Beckman Coulter, UK) at 25 degC temperature. CS-PLHNs were diluted properly (1:10) with DDW prior to measurement. All measurements were conducted using He-Ne laser generated scattering light at a fixed angle of 165deg. The electrophoretic mobility of CS-PLHNs, under the influence of an applied electric field was measured for determination of zeta potential.
2.4.2 Encapsulation efficiency (EE). The EE (%) of prepared CS-PLHNs was measured spectrophotometrically (Shimadzu UV 1800, Japan) at lmax of 239 nm by estimating the free CS content in supernatant (indirect method). CS-PLHNs were centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 15 minutes at 4 degC using cooling centrifuge. The clear supernatant was decanted, appropriately diluted and analyzed for free CS content.The EE of CS-PLHNs was calculated using the following formula:
2.4.3 Fourier transforms infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). The FTIR spectra of CS, PLGA, lecithin, PVA, physical mixture and CS-PLHNs were recorded using FTIR spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU 8400, Japan) in order to observe any significant change, if occurs, between CS and components of CS-PLHNs, both after and before the encapsulation of CS. The scanning was performed over the wavenumber ranging from 4000 to 400 cm-1 at room temperature and the resolution was set at 4 cm-1.
2.4.4 Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). The physical state of CS inside PLHNs and compatibility of CS with components of PLHNs were assessed by DSC. The thermograms were recorded on TGA/DSC-1, Star system (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland), which was previously calibrated with indium. Weighed samples were sealed in an aluminium crimp cell and purged with air (flow rate of 40 ml min-1). A temperature was raised at a rate of 10 degC min-1 and heat flow was recorded over the scanning temperature range from 50 to 300 degC under nitrogen atmosphere (purging rate of 10 ml min-1).
2.4.5 Powder X-ray diffractometry (PXRD). The PXRD pattern of CS, PLGA, lecithin, PVA, physical mixture and CS-PLHNs were obtained on Rigaku portable X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) for characterizing their crystallographic structure as well as changing in physical state of CS during the preparation. The X-ray radiation source was Cu Ka 1 tube operated at a voltage of 40 kV and 25 mA current. The diffraction patterns were recorded over the 2th scanning range from 5deg to 50deg with a step size 0.01deg at 2deg min-1 scanning speed.
2.4.6 Shape and surface morphology.
2.4.6.1 High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). The core-shell structure and shape of the CS-PLHNs were examined by employing transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Briefly, a drop of appropriately diluted CS-PLHNs was placed on the carbon-coated copper grid and stained with 2% w/v phosphotungstic acid. The excess of dispersion was wicked off with filter paper and allowed to dry in air. Then, the grid was examined using TEM (TECHNAI-20G2, Philips, Holland) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV with different magnifications.
2.4.6.2 Atomic force microscopy (AFM). The surface morphology of CS-PLHNs was visualized by atomic force microscope (NT-MDT, Moscow, Russia). Briefly, a drop of CS-PLHNs was deposited on freshly cleaved mica and allowed to immobilize for 1-2 min, subsequently air dried to form thin film. Then, surface morphology of CS-PLHNs was visualized by mounting on AFM scanner and imaged in tapping mode with scanning rate of 0.5 Hz. Dimensional analysis was performed using SOLVER NEXT software.
2.4.6.3 Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). For this study, FITC tagged micron sized CS-PLHNs were intentionally prepared by using similar procedure, except for sonication step. Thus, the obtained micron sized CS-PLHNs were visualized for their core-shell structure using confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM 510 META, Carl Zeiss Inc., USA). In this, study FITC tagged PLGA was used to get fluorescent core.26
2.5 In vitro drug release study
The in vitro drug release of prepared CS-PLHNs was determined using modified dialysis bag diffusion technique. The CS-PLHNs dispersion (3 ml equivalent to 10 mg of CS) was placed in dialysis bag (MWCO 12-14 kDa). After that, both ends of dialysis bag were fastened and immersed in 150 ml of phosphate buffer pH 7.4 containing flask, maintained at 37 +- 0.5 degC under 100 rpm speed. An aliquot of 5 ml of sample was withdrawn at regular time intervals and were replaced with equal volume of fresh media in order to maintain sink condition throughout experimentation. Withdrawn samples were filtered through 0.2 m syringe filters and suitability diluted for analyzing drug content spectrophotometrically at lmax of 239 nm. In vitro drug release data were further fitted to various release kinetic models, including zero-order model, first-order model, Higuchi model and Korsemeyer-Peppas model, in order to analyze the kinetics and mechanism of drug release. The correlation coefficient (R2) was determined by regression analysis to determine the best fitting of model.27
2.6 Stability study
2.6.1 Stability study in simulated biological milieu. CS-PLHNs were evaluated for their stability in the different biological media i.e., simulated gastric fluid (SGF, pH 1.2) and simulated intestinal fluid (SIF, pH 6.8) in order to assess the influence of various GIT fluid pH condition on their properties. Briefly, 250 ml of CS-PLHNs were diluted up to 10 ml of different simulated fluids and incubated at 37 degC for different time interval. At the end of the incubation period, CS-PLHNs were analyzed for particle size, PDI and EE for determining their stability in simulated biological milieu.
2.6.2 Accelerated and real time storage stability study. The stability of CS-PLHNs was assessed over a period of 6 month at room temperature (25 +- 2 degC), refrigerated condition (4 +- 1 degC), and accelerated condition (40 +- 2 degC/75 +- 5% RH) as per ICH guideline. Lyophilized CS-PLHNs were sealed in glass vials and placed in stability chamber at different environmental conditions. Samples were withdrawn at different time intervals (0, 1, 2, 3 and 6 months) and analyzed in terms of particle size, PDI and EE.
2.7 Animal studies
2.7.1 Animals. The animal study protocol was duly approved by Central Animal Ethical Committee of Banaras Hindu University (no. Dean/2014/CAEC/856). All the animal studies were carried out in accordance with the guidelines of CPCSEA (Committee for the Purpose of Control and Supervision of Experiments on Animal), Ministry of Social Justice and Empowerment, Government of India, New Delhi. Adult Charles Foster (CF) rats (225 +- 20 g) of either sex were used for all the animal studies. Animals were housed in propylene cages under standard laboratory conditions with a 12 h light/dark cycle for one week before the experiment. They had free access to standard rodent diet with water ad libitum. Animals were fasted overnight prior to 12 h of experiment but had free access to water. Animals were sacrificed by euthanasia, and disposal of the used animals was done by burning them using an incinerator. All efforts were made in order to reduce animal suffering.
2.7.2 Ex vivo intestinal permeation study. The permeation potential of CS-PLHNs across the GIT was assessed by ex vivo intestinal permeation study, using non-everted gut sac technique. Animals were sacrificed by cervical dislocation under excessive ether anesthesia. The small intestine was isolated by abdominal incision and immediately placed into bubbled (carbogen, 95:5 O2/CO2) cold saline solution. Ileum was flushed with physiological saline solution to remove any intestinal content and cut into segments. The CS solution (1 ml, 10 mg ml-1) and CS-PLHNs (1 ml, equivalent to 10 mg ml-1) were filled in intestinal sac and tied on both sides. Each filled intestinal sac was mounted in 40 ml phosphate buffer pH 7.4 filled conical flasks, maintained at 37 +- 0.5 degC with continuous aeration. Samples were collected from receptor compartment at different time interval and replaced with same volume of fresh buffer. Withdrawn samples were filtered through 0.2 m syringe filters and suitability diluted in order to analyze CS content spectrophotometrically at lmax of 239 nm.28,29 Apparent permeability coefficients (Papp) were calculated using following formula and expressed in cm s-1.
where Q/t is the rate of CS-PLHNs/CS appearance in the receiver compartment, A is the exposed surface area of intestinal tissue (cm2) and Co is the concentration of the CS in the donor compartment at zero time.Permeability enhancement ratio was calculated according to following formula.
2.7.3 In vivo intestinal uptake study using CLSM. The intestinal particulate uptake and thereby, permeation of CS-PLHNs was visualized by CLSM upon oral administration in rats. FITC tagged CS-PLHNs were administered orally (20 mg kg-1) using oral gavage to overnight fasted rats. After 2 h of oral administration, rats were sacrificed. Small intestine was removed by abdominal incision, washed with physiological saline solution and preserved with 10% neutral buffered formalin solution till cryosectioning. Tissue samples were freezed by embedding in cryostat media (OCT) and the intestinal transverse-sections of 5 mm thickness were sliced, using the cryo-microtome (Leica CM1850, Leica Microsystem srl, Milan, Italy) at -20 degC. The CS-PLHNs uptake and permeation in the intestinal tissue was observed by visualizing slides under a LSM 510 META confocal microscope (Carl Zeiss Inc., USA) at different magnification with excitation wavelength of 488 nm.29
2.7.4 In vivo pharmacokinetic study. Rats were randomly divided into three groups (n = 6 rats per group) for single dose oral bioavailability study. Each group received formulations orally at a dose of 20 mg kg-1 body weight of animal with dose volume of 500 ml, for maintaining the dose constant. First group received CS solution whereas second group was administered with CS-PLHNs for relative pharmacokinetic profiling. Third group served as control, which received only saline solution. Serial blood samples (250 ml) were withdrawn from retro-orbital plexus at different time intervals, under mild ether anesthesia and collected into heparinized micro-centrifuge tubes. The blood loss was compensated by injecting equivalent volume of normal saline solution followed by blood sampling. Plasma was separated after blood collection by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 5 min at -4 degC and frozen at -20 degC till further analysis. Drug analysis in plasma samples was carried out by the earlier reported and in-house validated RP-HPLC (Shimadzu LC 20 AD, Japan) method.30 The HPLC system (Shimadzu Corporation, Koyoto, Japan) equipped with C-18 reverse phase column (250 mm x 4.6 mm, PS 5 m), a degasser (DGU-20A3) and a pump (LC-20AD) with UV-Visible detector (SPD-20A) was utilized for the quantification of the CS in plasma samples. The samples were analyzed at 239 nm wavelength using isocratic elution at a flow rate of 1 ml min-1. Mobile phase consisted of acetonitrile and 20 mM potassium dihydrogen orthophosphate of pH 2.0 in the ratio of 75:25 was utilized for analysis. The mobile phase was filtered through 0.45 m Millipore membrane filter before use. Various pharmacokinetic parameters were determined using non-compartmental analysis of plasma concentration-time profile data through Winnonlin(r) 6.1 (Pharsight Corporation, Mountain View, CA) pharmacokinetic program.
2.8 Statistical analysis
GraphPad Prism software (version 5.03, GraphPad Software, USA) was used for statistical comparisons of the results to control. Statistical analysis of the results were performed using student (unpaired) t-test and one-way ANOVA test. All the results were presented as mean +- standard deviation (SD), except in vivo study, in which results were expressed as mean +- standard error of mean (SEM). The differences were assumed statistical significant when p < 0.05 (95% confidence interval).

The significance influence of independent variables (i.e., lipid/polymer ratio (X1), concentration of surfactant (X2) and organic phase/aqueous phase ratio (X3) and concentration of polymer (X4)) on response variables (i.e., particle size (Y1), encapsulation efficiency (EE) (Y2) and PDI (Y3)) was determined by employing RSM. According to design matrix of BBD for 4-factor at 3-level, a total of 29 batches of CS-PLHNs were prepared and the response data are summarized in Table 2. The statistical evaluation of the results was conducted by applying ANOVA through Design-Expert(r) software. All the response variables were fitted to different regression models. The fitting of the best model was confirmed by highest F-value. The magnitude of the effect of independent variable is denoted by positive and negative sign corresponding to their direct and indirect effect, respectively. Further, response surface 3D plots and contour plots were developed in order to delineate the interactive effect of any two independent variables on response variable as depicted in Fig. 1. The useful results obtained by statistical analysis of data reaffirmed the utility of "Quality by Design" approach in performing experiments.24
Table 2 Box-Behnken experimental design representing experimental runs with independent variables and their observed responses: particle size (Y1), encapsulation efficiency (Y2), and PDI (Y3) of CS-PLHNsa
3.1.1 Influence of independent variables on particle size. The various factor level combinations as per design matrix of BBD, resulted in particle size of CS-PLHNs vary from a minimum of 216.3 +- 2.4 nm to maximum of 342.2 +- 11.2 nm. The observed wide variation in response variable indicates that the particle size was strongly affected by the selected formulation variables. The quadratic statistical polynomial equation, presented below was generated by response surface analysis, which quantified the empirical relationship between the particle size and significant independent variables.
The quadratic model with F-value of 58.50 (p < 0.0001) implied that the model was significant for fitting to the results of particle size. The high R2 value (0.9832) indicated the existence of good correlation between the predicted results and the experimental results. The minimal difference between predicted R2 (0.9080) and adjusted R2 (0.9664) value suggested the adequacy of the selected model for the prediction of response. The F-value for lack of fit of the model was 5.46 suggested that the model allows satisfactory fitting to data with non-significant lack of fit (p = 0.0581). Further, low value for coefficient of variation (2.47%) indicated high degree of precision and reliability of the model. Hence, this quadratic model could be used to navigate the design space.23
Statistical analysis revealed that lipid/polymer ratio (X1) and concentration of polymer (X4) affects positively whereas concentration of surfactant (X2) and organic phase/aqueous phase ratio (X3) provides negative effect on particle size (Fig. 1(A)-(D)). The lipid/polymer ratio (X1) appeared to be one of the most dominant variables amongst all, which positively influences particle size owing to its direct effect on viscosity. The significant increment in particle size at higher lipid/polymer ratio could be attributed to the reduction of the shearing efficiency of stirrer at higher viscosity which resulted in thick covering of the polymeric core by lipid coat. Additionally, higher lipid/polymer ratio also favors the formation of liposome due to the presence of high amount of lipid, above its critical micellar concentration and coexistence of liposomes with PLHNs might be responsible for measuring the enhanced particle size.18 Contrary, the remarkable drop off in the particle size was noticed at higher surfactant concentration (X2), which might be due to reduction of the interfacial tension between the dispersed phase and dispersion medium.31 Moreover, high surfactant concentration also offers droplet stabilization against coalescence during the emulsification process by interfacial localization and protects from impinging with each other in order to grow into bigger ones.32 Similarly, organic phase/aqueous phase ratio (X3) showed inverse relationship with particle size. This could be attributed to the fact that large amount of organic solvent would decrease the viscosity and thereby, impart high shear stress, which would result in breakdown of emulsion droplets without any coalescence.23,33 The particle size increased significantly with increase in polymer concentration (X4) might be because of triggering of the polymer-polymer interaction coupled with viscosity of the dispersed phase, which would result in formation of coarse dispersion with marked density difference. According to Stoke's low, the resultant density difference propels particle collisions and thereby, aggregations which are difficult to be broken up using the same energy of mixing.31,33
3.1.2 Influence of independent variables on encapsulation efficiency. The EE of developed CS-PLHNs was found to be varying in the range from 51.3 +- 2.4% to 63.6 +- 1.6% for different formulation variable combinations. The second-order polynomial equation relating the EE (Y2) and independent variables, generated by multiple linear regressions can be represented as follows
Based on the F-test in the proposed quadratic model, the model with F-value of 27.80 (p < 0.0001) implied that the quadratic model was statistically significant for relating the results of EE. The value of correlation coefficient for EE was found to be 0.9653, indicating good correlation. The higher value of adjusted R2 (0.9306) and predicted R2 (0.8323) with little difference confirmed the adequacy of the model with high significance. Additionally, the suitability of the model with excellent fitting to data was demonstrated by the higher F-value (1.40) which implied that the lack of fit was non-significant (p = 0.3977). Further, lower value of coefficient of variation (1.76%) indicated excellent precision and reliability of the regression model. All the above consideration indicated that the present regression model could be used to navigate the design space.23
As depicted in Fig. 1(E)-(H), the lipid/polymer ratio (X1) was found to increase EE of CS-PLHNs. Perhaps high lipid/polymer ratio provided thick lipidic barrier surrounding the polymeric core, which would have curtailed the diffusion of the drug towards the external aqueous phase.19 Conversely, EE was found to decrease with an increase in surfactant concentration (X2). This could be explained by the fact that the higher surfactant concentration increases drug solubility by reducing interfacial tension and increasing partition of drug into aqueous phase, which in turn substantially lowers EE.34,35 The organic phase/aqueous phase ratio (X3) was also negatively related to EE, probably as a result of enhanced partition of the drug into aqueous phase due to decreased viscosity.35 The reduction in viscosity facilitates rapid diffusion of drug during emulsification and evaporation step owing to high shear stress, which ultimately results in CS-PLHNs with low payload.23 However, concentration of polymer (X4) showed positive effect on EE of CS-PLHNs, which accordant with published literature that higher EE was obtained with higher polymer concentration due to rise in viscosity as well as faster solidification.34 Additionally, increased polymer concentration would increase diffusion path length by forming larger sized CS-PLHNs and also form interfacial viscous diffusion barrier, which would further favor the encapsulation by restricting the drug diffusion.36
3.1.3 Influence of independent variables on polydispersity index. The PDI of CS-PLHNs varied from minimum of 0.141 +- 0.099 to maximum of 0.389 +- 0.024, for various factor level combinations. The quadratic model proposed the following second-order polynomial equation describing the relationship between PDI (Y3) and independent variables
The quadratic model was found to be statistically significant with F-value of 71.04 (p < 0.0001) for fitting the results of PDI. The high R2 coefficient (0.9861) suggested the goodness of fit of the model for predicting the results and over 98% of the total variations are well explained by the regression model. The adequacy of the selected regression model was supported by the minimal difference between predicted R2 (0.9239) and adjusted R2 (0.9722) value. The non-significant lack of fit (F-value = 5.62; p = 0.0554) confirmed the suitability of the model with satisfactory fitting to data. Additionally, low value for coefficient of variation (5.13%) indicated the reliability of the quadratic model with adequate precision. Therefore, the proposed model could be employed in order to navigate the design space.23
The influence of the independent variables on PDI can be evaluated by observing the Fig. 1(I)-(L). The lipid/polymer ratio (X1) exhibited significant positive influence on PDI owing to its direct impact on the thickness of the lipid covering over the polymeric core. Higher lipid/polymer ratio would accelerate the aggregation by suppressing their native surface charge, which would result uneven size distribution. Moreover, formation of single and multilamellar liposomes at higher lipid concentration also enforces polydispersity.23 Nevertheless, significant drop in polydispersity was noticed at elevated surfactant concentration (X2) and organic phase/aqueous phase ratio (X3), could be attributed to the marked reduction in the interfacial tension, as reported earlier. The reduction in interfacial tension between aqueous phase and organic phase ensures good emulsification process, imparting homogeneity to CS-PLHNs.27 The polymer concentration (X4) exhibited positive relationship with PDI on account of its direct impact on emulsification efficiency. Higher polymer concentration could favor the formation of coarse dispersion due to lack of sufficient energy to overcome the viscous forces as well as lack of sufficient surfactant, which could ultimately impose the polydispersity.37
3.1.4 Optimization of CS-PLHNs. Optimization of formulation by considering all the objectives at a time is difficult because of opposite effect of various independent variables. The optimum level of one independent variable might result in an inverse effect for other independent variable. Hence, a numerical optimization technique using desirability function was probed for simultaneous optimization of the independent variables, which would yield CS-PLHNs with desired quality traits. The constraints were fixed for all four independent variables in the Design-Expert(r) software in order to achieve the CS-PLHNs, which would have maximum EE with minimum particle size and PDI value. The optimized CS-PLHNs were formulated using predicted levels of independent variables for confirming the validity as well as predictive capability of BBD as shown in Table 3. The desirability of the optimized CS-PLHNs was 0.866. The good agreement with low percentage of bias between predicted results and experimental results reaffirmed the reliability of BBD for statistical optimization.38 The optimized CS-PLHNs showed the EE of 57.8 +- 1.32%, which was significantly higher than that reported earlier. Moreover, for comparison, the simple polymeric nanoparticles were prepared by using the same composition and same method as optimized PLHNs without using lipid, which showed EE of 24.2 +- 0.5%. The significant difference (p < 0.05) clearly indicates the utility of lipid envelop for enhancing the EE of hydrophilic drug molecule by forming PLHNs.
Table 3 Optimal parameters with its desirability value generated by Design expert(r) for CS-PLHNsa
3.2 FTIR spectroscopy
In order to examine the interaction between CS and components of PLHNs as well as for ensuring the encapsulation of CS inside the CS-PLHNs, primary concerns were taken using FTIR study. The FTIR spectra of CS, PLGA, lecithin, PVA, physical mixture and CS-PLHNs are depicted in Fig. 2. The CS spectrum exhibited two basic characteristic peaks at 1640 cm-1 and 3416 cm-1 indicative of CO stretching and O-H stretching, respectively. The peaks at 2880 cm-1, 1477 cm-1, 1573 cm-1 and 1410 cm-1 are assigned to the alkane C-H, aromatic C-H, asymmetric and symmetric COO-, respectively. Additionally, large number of characteristic absorption bands in fingerprint region (1400-600 cm-1), were noticed as a result of vibration within the molecule.5 FTIR spectrum of PLGA showed that the significant peaks of PLGA are CO stretching at 1748 cm-1, C-O stretching at 1194 cm-1, C-H stretching at 2948 cm-1 and broad bands between 3200-3600 cm-1 due to its terminal hydroxyl group.27 In case of FTIR spectrum of PVA, peaks in the range of 2840-3000 cm-1 for C-H stretching, 3200-3550 cm-1 for O-H stretching, 1290 cm-1 for C-N stretching and 1750-1735 cm-1 for CO as well as C-O stretching from acetate group were observed. FTIR spectrum of lecithin showed characteristic band in the range of 1765-1720 cm-1 corresponding to the CO vibration and another band in the range of 1200-1145 cm-1 due to the PO2 vibration. Additionally, some bands in the range of 1200-970 cm-1 were noticed corresponding to both P-O-C as well as PO2 vibrations, respectively. All the major peaks pertaining to CS were retained in the FTIR spectrum of physical mixture, suggesting the non-involvement of CS in any kind of physicochemical interaction with the components of PLHNs. Further, FTIR spectrum of CS-PLHNs showed the appearance of all the characteristic peaks of CS with little variations in frequencies similar to its pure form, confirming the successful encapsulation of CS inside the PLHNs without any change in its chemical nature during the preparation process.
3.3 DSC study
DSC thermogram of CS, PLGA, lecithin, PVA, their physical mixture and CS-PLHNs are depicted in Fig. 3. In the DSC thermogram of pure CS, sharp melting endotherm appeared at 264 degC attributed to its melting point, indicating its crystalline behaviour. PLGA and PVA exhibited sharp endothermic peak at 52 degC and 226 degC, respectively. Lecithin displayed broad endothermic peak at 87 degC corresponding to its phase transition. The thermogram of physical mixture of CS with the components of PLHNs also showed the similar thermal behaviour of CS at 264 degC as appeared in pure CS thermogram. The physical mixture also showed the characteristic endothermic peaks of all the components of the PLHNs without any considerable shift in peak positions, confirming the compatibility of CS with each other. Contrary, the absence of characteristic melting endotherm of CS in the thermogram of CS-PLHNs suggested that the phase transformation from crystalline state to amorphous state has taken place, during the encapsulation process.39 The shear stress provided by the stirrer during the fabrication process of PLHNs might have prevented the recrystallization of CS from its tiny droplets, leaving CS in an amorphous molecular dispersion form inside the PLHNs. Furthermore, the absence of redundant endothermic peaks in the thermogram of CS-PLHNs confirmed the homogeneous encapsulation of CS inside the PLHNs without any interactions.40
3.4 PXRD study
PXRD patterns of pure CS, PLGA, lecithin, PVA, physical mixture and CS-PLHNs are depicted in Fig. 4. CS displayed characteristic distinctive peaks at 2th angles of 8deg, 9.83deg, 11.5deg, 14deg, 16.9deg, 19.7deg, 24.3deg, 26.6deg and numerous minor peaks up to 35deg. The intense, sharp and well resolved peaks indicated that CS was highly crystalline in nature.41 The diffraction patterns of the PLGA, PVA and lecithin exhibited number of small diffuse peaks with broad halo. The diffractogram of physical mixture showed all the sharp peaks of the CS, nearly at the same scattering 2th angles as appeared in pure form, indicating the retention of its crystalline behaviour without any physical change. Oppositely, the PXRD pattern of CS-PLHNs showed that all the characteristic sharp peaks of CS were abridged in their intensity and converted into broad, diffuse peaks. The stifling of diffraction intensity of CS confirmed that the physical state of CS was transformed towards an amorphous molecular dispersion form upon encapsulation inside the PLHNs.42
3.5 Morphology study
The morphological study using HR-TEM micrographs (Fig. 5(A)) revealed that CS-PLHNs were discrete, homogeneous and spherical in shape having uniform size distribution. High magnification indicated that CS-PLHNs were appeared as spherical particles which have an inner dark polymeric core surrounded by the lighter phospholipid envelop. The core-shell structure of CS-PLHNs was clearly visible due to the marked electron density difference between polymer and lipid.18 The surface of CS-PLHNs was observed as smooth without any ruptures. The particle size obtained with TEM micrographs was comparable to that obtained by particle size analyzer. Additionally, electron diffraction (ED) pattern of CS-PLHNs was generated by HR-TEM in order to confirm the physical state of CS inside the PLHNs. The ED pattern (Fig. 5(D)) exhibited smooth diffraction halo pattern with absence of star shaped, bright spots in its diffraction ring pattern, which confirmed the encapsulation of CS as an amorphous dispersion form inside the PLHNs,39 further substantiating the inference deduced from DSC and XRD results.
The surface morphology of CS-PLHNs was further determined by AFM study in order to confirm the morphological aspect revealed by HR-TEM. The topographic and three-dimensional (3D) AFM micrographs of CS-PLHNs, generated by the atomic level interaction between a sharp tip and the surface of CS-PLHNs are depicted in Fig. 5(B) and (E). The particle size and surface morphology determined by AFM was in accordance to HR-TEM study. The particle size of CS-PLHNs, measured by AFM was almost below 250 nm. AFM images showed uniform spherical shaped CS-PLHNs having smooth surface without any visible crevices or pores. The probable reason for smooth surface could be the uniform covering of lipid over the surface of polymeric core, which has resealed the pores generated by the diffusion of organic solvent during the preparation.
Further, core-shell type of architecture of CS-PLHNs was confirmed by CLSM study. The CLSM micrograph of fluorescent CS-PLHNs, generated by visualizing under confocal microscope showed clear differentiation of two regions of different material (Fig. 5(C) and (F)). The green color represented the polymeric core whereas very light circle surrounding the green color represented the colorless lipid envelop, which corroborates well with the findings of HR-TEM study.
3.6 In vitro drug release study
In vitro drug release study was performed in order to assess the potential of CS-PLHNs to control the release of CS for prolonging the action. The in vitro drug release profile of CS from CS-PLHNs, in phosphate buffer pH 7.4 is presented in Fig. 6. The CS release from CS-PLHNs showed phasic release behaviour, comprising of initial burst release followed by extended release over a period of 48 h. The superclass II release was noticed up to 1 h with an initial burst of nearly 20% of the drug. This might likely be due to the rapid diffusion and desorption of surface adsorbed or weakly bounded drug as well as partial erosion of the outer phospholipid layer of CS-PLHNs, which resulted in quicker diffusion of hydrophilic drug as reported earlier.42 The subsequent extended release was achieved up to 48 h, indicating the slower diffusion of the encapsulated CS from CS-PLHNs. The sustained release might be ascribed to the lipidic barrier provided by phospholipid envelop, which has restricted the penetration of release medium and curtailed the faster immobilization of the CS from the polymeric core and thereby, extended the release.20 Further, drug release mechanism and kinetics was determined by substituting the drug release profile data of CS-PLHNs to the different release kinetic models. The release kinetic modeling showed that the drug release pattern from the CS-PLHNs was best explained by Higuchi model (highest R2 value), indicating matrix diffusion controlled release process (Table 4). Additionally, the fitting of Korsmeyer-Peppas semi empirical model revealed the value of release exponent (n), which was found to be 0.493, confirming the Fickian diffusion based release pattern of CS-PLHNs (n < 0.5 for Fickian diffusion). This is expected since the CS being hydrophilic drug, has a greater tendency for diffusion.27,39 Hence, it is possible to achieve fluctuation free steady state CS plasma level using CS-PLHNs, which provides loading dose followed by maintenance dose for extended period of time.
Table 4 Release kinetic models for simulation of release behaviour of CS from CS-PLHNs in phosphate buffer pH 7.4
3.7 Stability study
3.7.1 Stability study in simulated biological milieu. Table 5 shows the influence of different simulated biological media on properties of CS-PLHNs after incubation at 37 degC. The significant change (p < 0.05) in the particle size and PDI value of CS-PLHNs was observed upon incubation with SGF of pH 1.2, which might be due to aggregation of particles, offered by lecithin envelop in harsh acidic environment. However, EE remained unchanged due to the acidic nature of drug as well as barrier provided by the lipid envelop, which might have prevented the direct exposure of drug molecule to external harsh environment and prevented drug loss. Whereas CS-PLHNs did not show any significant change (p > 0.05) in their physical attributes, upon incubation with SIF of pH 6.8, which might be due to protection offered by lipidic shell against digestive enzyme of intestine. Interestingly, CS-PLHNs incubated with SGF and subsequent shifted in SIF, did not show any significant change in particle size and EE. However, PDI was increased, which might be due to partial uncovering of the lipidic shell as a result of drastic pH change during the transferring from SGF to SIF. Hence, CS-PLHNs may certain to maintain their attributes during their gastric transit.
Table 5 Stability of CS-PLHNs in different simulated biological milieua
3.7.2 Accelerated and real time storage stability study. Storage stability study was performed at different environmental conditions in order to assess the potential of CS-PLHNs to withstand against environmental changes. Fig. 7 depicts the physicochemical attributes of the CS-PLHNs during the stability study. The physical appearance of the CS-PLHNs did not show any significant difference (p > 0.05) at different conditions. Additionally, insignificant change (p > 0.05) in the particle size, PDI and EE was noticed for CS-PLHNs, stored at room temperature (25 +- 2 degC) and refrigerated condition (4 +- 1 degC) over a period of 6 month. However, significant difference (p < 0.05) in the particle size, PDI and EE was observed for the CS-PLHNs stored at accelerated condition (40 +- 2 degC/75 +- 5% RH), indicating the instability of the formulation, which might be due to conglomeration of the CS-PLHNs, offered by lecithin molecules and degradation of the polymer. Hence, it was recommended that developed CS-PLHNs should be stored at below room temperature (<25 degC) or at refrigerated condition (4 +- 1 degC), in order to retain their pharmaceutical properties for safe and effective long-term use.
3.8 Ex vivo permeation study
The ex vivo permeation study using non-everted rat intestinal model was accomplished in order to assess the permeation potential of CS-PLHNs. The significant improvement in the intestinal permeation of CS, almost 6 folds (p < 0.05) was observed by encapsulating inside PLHNs compared to CS solution. Fig. 8 represents the intestinal permeation profile of CS from CS solution and CS-PLHNs, along with their apparent permeability coefficient (Papp). The Papp value for CS from CS solution and CS-PLHNs were found to be 0.909 (+-0.049) x 10-5 cm s-1 and 5.441 (+-0.373) x 10-5 cm s-1, respectively. The high hydrophilicity offered by the tween carboxylic group of the CS might be responsible for its poor permeation across rat intestine.5 The observed significant high (p < 0.05) permeation of CS in the form of CS-PLHNs at each time point might be attributed to their nano-size as well as hydrophobic nature of lipid, which would have facilitated the absorption across intestine through specialized transport mechanisms.14 It has been previously reported that the nanoparticles of less than 500 nm are effectively absorbed via endocytosis through lymphoid tissues in the intestine.10 Additionally, the enhanced interaction of CS-PLHNs with lipophilic biological membrane due to phospholipid envelop, might be also plausible reason for an effective endocytosis through enterocytes as well as M cells of PP, which eventually reflected via higher CS concentration in basolateral side.19,43 Results are in well corroboration with the hypothesis.
3.9 In vivo intestinal uptake study
The in vivo intestinal permeation potential of the CS-PLHNs was visualized using confocal microscopy after oral administration in the rats. The confocal microscopic images of the cross sections of rat intestine, showing the absorption of CS-PLHNs are portrayed in Fig. 9. The strong green fluorescence was noticed throughout the apical portion of the villi in the intestinal lumen, specifically mucosa and submucosa (blood and lymphatic vessels). The various stage of internalization and distribution of CS-PLHNs in the enterocytes and intestinal mucosa after 2 h of administration suggested that, an effective endocytosis was taken place in the intestinal mucosal epithelia, PP of the follicle-associated epithelium and the underneath regions of the secondary lymphoid organ. The results confirmed that the CS-PLHNs were able to interact with intestinal barrier and be internalized by following various transportation mechanisms such as paracellular pathway, transcellular pathway through enterocytes and endocytosis by M-cells of PP (Fig. 9(III)).44-46 Additionally, a strong fluorescence was also observed at the surface of the villi conceivably as a consequence of CS-PLHNs' bioadhesion with the mucous layer of GIT wall. The mucoadhesivity imparted by the phospholipid envelop and surfactant layer (i.e., PVA) of CS-PLHNs, prolonged the residence time and thereby, improved the intestinal permeation.10,43,45 As CS remains encapsulated within the CS-PLHNs during the internalization process across GIT, it resulted in enhanced permeation, which further strengthens our hypothesis. However, specific mechanism needs to be elucidated by further studies.
3.10 In vivo pharmacokinetic study
The plasma drug concentration time profiles obtained after oral administration of CS solution and CS-PLHNs are presented in Fig. 10, and the corresponding various pharmacokinetic parameters are summarized in Table 6. The significant difference (p < 0.05) was noticed in the pharmacokinetic parameters of CS solution and CS-PLHNs, after non-compartmental analysis of CS plasma concentration time profile. The peak plasma concentration (Cmax) of 268.20 +- 9.59 ng ml-1 was observed for CS-PLHNs after single dose administration, which was 2.4 times higher than those obtained for the CS solution (112.23 +- 5.90 ng ml-1). The hydrophobic surface as well as nano-size of the CS-PLHNs could have facilitated the permeation of CS across GIT, by virtue of their specific absorption mechanisms and that might be responsible for the higher value of Cmax.14,39 The time taken to achieve maximum drug concentration (Tmax) was also 4 times delayed for CS-PLHNs as compared to CS solution, which confirmed the sustained in vivo CS release potential of CS-PLHNs similar to the in vitro release profile.44,47 The plasma level of CS declined after 1.52 +- 0.02 h, indicating rapid systemic elimination of the CS owing to its poor plasma protein binding, which was further evident by its low systemic mean residence time (MRT) of 2.79 +- 0.03 h. Whereas, 5.40 times and 4.76 times enhanced half-life (T1/2) and MRT, respectively was obtained with CS-PLHNs as compared to free CS solution, which might be due to extended absorption and slow release of CS from CS-PLHNs into systemic circulation.48 Moreover, the values of AUC0-48 h and AUC0- for CS-PLHNs were also significantly improved (p < 0.05) compared to pure CS solution. From the various pharmacokinetic parameters, it was clear that CS-PLHNs showed the promising potential for oral delivery of CS with approximately 11.9 fold higher relative bioavailability, compared to pure CS solution. The significant improvement (p < 0.05) in oral bioavailability of CS, achieved with CS-PLHNs might be resulted due to the enhanced systemic absorption of CS-PLHNs through specialized absorption mechanisms across GIT such as paracellular transport, transcellular transport via enterocytes and M-cells of the PP (Fig. 9(III)),44,47 which corroborates well with the ex vivo and in vivo intestinal uptake studies and further substantiates the hypothesis. Furthermore, phospholipid covering over the polymeric core might have improved the bioadhesion of CS-PLHNs with the intestinal membrane and assisted in the CS-PLHNs movement through paracellular pathway.42,43
Table 6 Pharmacokinetic parameters of CS following single dose oral administration of CS solution and CS-PLHNs in rats (dose: 20 mg kg-1)a
